objectives.text: Set sampler primitive options such as resilience levels
Bypass runtime error mitigations and implement your own
Understand the theoretical background behind the sampler primitive

questions.stem: You have transpiled a circuit for your backend, producing the ISA circuit `isa_qc`, and created `sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)`. Which call correctly submits the circuit for execution?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

# isa_qc: circuit already transpiled for `backend`
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
job = ...  # which call?

options.text: `job = sampler.run([isa_qc])`
`job = sampler.run(isa_qc)`
`job = sampler.run([isa_qc], backend)`
`job = sampler.run([isa_qc], backend=backend)`

explanation.correct: SamplerV2.run() takes an iterable of PUBs (Primitive Unified Blocs). A bare ISA circuit is a valid PUB-like element, but it must be wrapped in a list: `sampler.run([isa_qc])`. The backend is not passed to run() — it was already bound via `mode=` when the primitive was constructed.

distractors.B: V1 primitives accepted bare circuits, but SamplerV2.run() requires an iterable of PUBs — passing the circuit unwrapped raises an error.
distractors.C: run() has no positional backend parameter — execution target is fixed by `mode=` at construction time; this raises a TypeError.
distractors.D: There is no `backend=` keyword on run() — that's a V1-migration misconception; the mode is set on the primitive itself.

evidence.A: call succeeded on fake_manila: returned job, result()[0].data.meas gave counts over 4 bitstrings, 1024 shots total
evidence.B: call raised ValueError: An invalid Sampler pub-like was given (<class 'qiskit._accelerate.circuit.CircuitInstruction'>). If you want to run a single circuit, you need to wrap it with `
evidence.C: call raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
evidence.D: call raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() got an unexpected keyword argument 'backend'

questions.stem: A Bell circuit is measured and submitted with `sampler.run([isa], shots=1000)`. What is the value of `sum(result[0].data.meas.get_counts().values())`?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

# isa: transpiled Bell circuit with measure_all()
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
result = sampler.run([isa], shots=1000).result()
counts = result[0].data.meas.get_counts()
print(sum(counts.values()))

options.text: `1000` — the counts across all outcomes always add up to the number of shots
`2` — a Bell state only produces the outcomes `00` and `11`
`500` — the shots are split evenly between the two measured qubits
`1024` — the sampler always uses its 1024 default shots

explanation.correct: get_counts() tallies every shot into its observed bitstring, so the counts sum to the total number of shots that were run. Here shots=1000 was passed explicitly, so the tallies sum to 1000 regardless of how many distinct bitstrings (2, 3, or 4) actually appear.

distractors.B: This confuses the number of *distinct outcomes* with the number of shots. get_counts() returns a dict whose keys are the distinct bitstrings but whose values sum to the shot count.
distractors.C: Shots are not divided among qubits — every shot measures the full register once, producing one bitstring; all 1000 bitstrings are tallied.
distractors.D: 1024 is only the default when no shots are supplied. Here `shots=1000` overrides the default, so exactly 1000 shots run.

evidence.A: option claims sum(counts.values())=1000; executed run gave 1000 -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims sum(counts.values())=2; executed run gave 1000 -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims sum(counts.values())=500; executed run gave 1000 -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims sum(counts.values())=1024; executed run gave 1000 -> match=False

questions.stem: After running a SamplerV2 job on a circuit that used `measure_all()`, you access `bits = result[0].data.meas`. Which statement about `bits` is correct?
questions.code:
result = sampler.run([isa], shots=256).result()
bits = result[0].data.meas

options.text: `bits` is a `BitArray` holding the per-shot measurement outcomes; call `bits.get_counts()` to summarize them into a dict
`bits` is already a dict that maps each bitstring to its count
`bits` is a NumPy array of measurement probabilities, one per basis state
`bits` is a `Counts` object, exactly like `result.get_counts()` returned in Qiskit V1

explanation.correct: SamplerV2 stores each register's outcomes in a BitArray inside the DataBin (`data.meas`). A BitArray keeps the raw per-shot bitstrings; you turn it into a summary histogram with `get_counts()` (dict) or read shots directly with `get_bitstrings()`. It is not itself the counts dict.

distractors.B: The counts dict is what `bits.get_counts()` *returns*; `bits` itself is the richer BitArray container of per-shot data, not the dict.
distractors.C: The Sampler returns samples, not a probability vector. Frequencies must be estimated by counting shots yourself; nothing in `data.meas` is a probability array.
distractors.D: This is a V1-migration misconception. V2 has no top-level `result.get_counts()`; results live in per-register BitArrays reached through `data.<register>`.

evidence.A: claim holds=True — type(bits).__name__='BitArray', isinstance(bits,dict)=False, get_counts() is dict=True
evidence.B: claim holds=False — isinstance(bits,dict)=False
evidence.C: claim holds=False — type name is 'BitArray', not ndarray
evidence.D: claim holds=False — type name is 'BitArray', not Counts

questions.stem: You build `sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)` and never touch its options, then call `sampler.run([isa])` with no `shots` argument. How many shots does the job run?
questions.code:
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
job = sampler.run([isa])   # no shots given, options untouched
num = job.result()[0].data.meas.num_shots

options.text: 1024 — the default value used when neither `shots` nor `default_shots` is set
4096 — the SamplerV2 default
1 — one shot unless you ask for more
It raises an error, because `shots` is a required argument

explanation.correct: When you pass neither a per-run `shots` value nor set `options.default_shots`, SamplerV2 falls back to its built-in default of 1024 shots. `shots` is optional precisely because this default exists.

distractors.B: 4096 is not the Sampler's default; the built-in fallback is 1024. This is a memorized-wrong-number distractor.
distractors.C: A single shot is never the default — sampling needs many repetitions to estimate a distribution. The fallback is 1024.
distractors.D: `shots` is optional. Omitting it is legal and simply uses the default (1024), so no error is raised.

evidence.A: claim holds=True — observed num_shots=1024, raised=False
evidence.B: claim holds=False — observed num_shots=1024, raised=False
evidence.C: claim holds=False — observed num_shots=1024, raised=False
evidence.D: claim holds=False — observed num_shots=1024, raised=False

questions.stem: This code raises an error when it reaches `sampler.run(...)`. What is the correct fix?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.h(0); qc.cx(0, 1); qc.ccx(0, 1, 2)
qc.measure_all()

sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)   # backend is a real/fake device
job = sampler.run([qc])             # <-- raises here

options.text: Transpile the circuit to an ISA circuit for the backend first (e.g. with `generate_preset_pass_manager`), then run the ISA circuit
Wrap the circuit in one more list: `sampler.run([[qc]])`
Just pass a shots count: `sampler.run([qc], shots=1024)`
Construct the sampler with no mode: `SamplerV2().run([qc])`

explanation.correct: Runtime primitives only accept ISA circuits — circuits whose gates and connectivity already match the backend's target. The raw circuit here uses gates (like the Toffoli) that are not in the backend basis, so `run` raises an input-validation error. Transpiling with a preset pass manager for the backend produces the ISA circuit that runs successfully.

distractors.B: The problem is not the list nesting — a single list of PUBs is already correct. Nesting changes nothing about the circuit still being non-ISA, so it still fails.
distractors.C: Adding `shots` does not make a non-ISA circuit acceptable; the validation error is about gates/connectivity, not the shot count.
distractors.D: Dropping the mode removes the execution target entirely and does not transpile anything; the circuit is still non-ISA and construction/run fails.

evidence.A: transpile->ISA then run: SUCCESS, counts sum=128
evidence.B: still raised IBMInputValueError: 'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target
evidence.C: still raised IBMInputValueError: 'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target
evidence.D: raised ValueError: A backend or session must be specified.

questions.stem: Hoping to turn on error mitigation for measurement counts, a developer writes `sampler.options.resilience_level = 1` on a `SamplerV2`. What actually happens?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
sampler.options.resilience_level = 1

options.text: It raises an error — `SamplerV2` has no `resilience_level`; that option belongs to `EstimatorV2`
It enables T-REx readout error mitigation on the sampled counts
It is accepted but silently ignored, because the sampler has no mitigation
It is an alias that turns on measurement twirling

explanation.correct: `resilience_level` is an EstimatorV2 option — resilience levels drive expectation-value mitigation such as TREX/ZNE. SamplerV2 exposes no `resilience_level`; assigning it raises a validation error ('no such attribute'). The Sampler's error-handling options are `dynamical_decoupling`, `twirling`, and execution/simulator settings — it returns raw samples and leaves mitigation to you.

distractors.B: Readout mitigation on expectation values (TREX) is reached through Estimator resilience settings, not through a Sampler option — the Sampler has none.
distractors.C: It is not silently ignored: the options object validates attributes and raises on an unknown one, so the line throws immediately.
distractors.D: There is no aliasing. Measurement twirling is set explicitly via `options.twirling.enable_measure`; `resilience_level` simply does not exist on the Sampler.

evidence.A:
setting resilience_level raised=True (ValidationError: 1 validation error for SamplerOptions
resilience_level
  Object has no attribute 'resilience_level' [type=no_such_attribute, input_value=1, ); hasattr=False
evidence.B: no silent success: raised=True
evidence.C: no silent success: raised=True
evidence.D: no silent success: raised=True

questions.stem: A 3-qubit circuit applies X to qubit 0 only, then measures qubit i into classical bit i. In the Sampler's counts, which bitstring dominates?
questions.code:
qc = QuantumCircuit(3, 3)
qc.x(0)                       # only qubit 0 is flipped
qc.measure(0, 0)
qc.measure(1, 1)
qc.measure(2, 2)
# ... transpile, then sampler.run([isa]) ...
counts = result[0].data.c.get_counts()

options.text: `'001'` — Qiskit prints bitstrings little-endian, so classical bit 0 (qubit 0) is the rightmost character
`'100'` — qubit 0 is the leftmost character in the bitstring
`'111'` — flipping any qubit flips the whole register
`'000'` — X on qubit 0 has no effect on the measured bits

explanation.correct: Qiskit uses little-endian bit ordering: in the printed bitstring the classical bit with the highest index is on the left and bit 0 is on the right. Only qubit 0 is excited, and it is measured into classical bit 0, so the rightmost character is 1 and the rest are 0 -> '001'.

distractors.B: This assumes big-endian ordering (qubit 0 leftmost). Qiskit is little-endian, so qubit 0 lands in the rightmost position, giving '001', not '100'.
distractors.C: X acts only on the qubit it targets; qubits 1 and 2 stay in |0>. The register does not collectively flip.
distractors.D: X on qubit 0 flips it to |1>, so its measured bit is 1, not 0 — the outcome cannot be all zeros.

evidence.A: option claims dominant bitstring '001'; observed argmax='001' (counts['001']=1706) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims dominant bitstring '100'; observed argmax='001' (counts['100']=11) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims dominant bitstring '111'; observed argmax='001' (counts['111']=0) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims dominant bitstring '000'; observed argmax='001' (counts['000']=111) -> match=False

questions.stem: A circuit has 2 parameters. You bind a NumPy array of shape (6, 2) and run one PUB: `sampler.run([(isa, vals)])`. What is `result[0].data.meas.shape`?
questions.code:
import numpy as np

# isa has 2 free parameters
vals = np.linspace(0, 1, 12).reshape(6, 2)  # shape (6, 2)
result = sampler.run([(isa, vals)]).result()
print(result[0].data.meas.shape)

options.text: `(6,)` — the trailing axis is consumed as the 2 parameters, leaving 6 broadcast results
`(6, 2)` — the result keeps the full shape of the parameter array
`(2,)` — one result per parameter
`()` — a single scalar result, since it is one PUB

explanation.correct: In a Sampler PUB the parameter array's last axis must have length = number of parameters; that axis is consumed to bind the circuit. The remaining leading shape becomes the result's broadcast shape. Shape (6, 2) with 2 parameters therefore yields 6 independent results -> data.meas.shape == (6,).

distractors.B: The parameter axis (the trailing 2) is consumed when binding; it does not survive into the result shape. Only the leading dimensions remain.
distractors.C: There are 6 parameter sets, not 2. The '2' is the number of parameters per set (the consumed axis), not the number of results.
distractors.D: One PUB with a broadcast parameter array still produces one result per parameter set. The shape is (6,), not a scalar; you index it as data.meas[i].

evidence.A: option claims data.meas.shape=(6,); executed run gave (6,) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims data.meas.shape=(6, 2); executed run gave (6,) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims data.meas.shape=(2,); executed run gave (6,) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims data.meas.shape=(); executed run gave (6,) -> match=False

questions.stem: A 2-parameter circuit is bound with a parameter array of shape (3, 4, 2) in a single PUB. What is the shape of `result[0].data.meas`?
questions.code:
import numpy as np

# isa has 2 free parameters
vals = np.zeros((3, 4, 2))   # a 3x4 grid of 2-parameter sets
result = sampler.run([(isa, vals)]).result()
print(result[0].data.meas.shape)

options.text: `(3, 4)` — the last axis (size 2) binds the parameters; the leading (3, 4) is the broadcast grid
`(3, 4, 2)` — the result mirrors the full input array shape
`(12,)` — broadcast results are always flattened to 1-D
`(2,)` — one result per parameter

explanation.correct: The trailing axis of a parameter array is reserved for the parameter values (here length 2 = the 2 parameters) and is consumed during binding. Everything before it is the broadcast shape, so a (3, 4, 2) array over a 2-parameter circuit produces a (3, 4) array of results, each indexable as data.meas[i, j].

distractors.B: The parameter axis is consumed, not retained. The result drops the trailing 2, leaving (3, 4).
distractors.C: Broadcast results preserve their multidimensional shape; they are not flattened. The grid stays (3, 4).
distractors.D: 2 is the number of parameters (the consumed axis), not the result count. There are 3x4 = 12 results arranged as (3, 4).

evidence.A: option claims shape=(3, 4); executed run gave (3, 4) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims shape=(3, 4, 2); executed run gave (3, 4) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims shape=(12,); executed run gave (3, 4) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims shape=(2,); executed run gave (3, 4) -> match=False

questions.stem: The circuit `isa` has exactly 2 free parameters. This call raises a `ValueError`. What fixes it?
questions.code:
import numpy as np

# isa has 2 free parameters (p[0], p[1])
vals = np.zeros((5, 3))
job = sampler.run([(isa, vals)])   # ValueError: inconsistent with last dimension

options.text: Give the array shape (5, 2) — the last axis must equal the number of parameters (2), producing 5 results
Transpose it to shape (3, 5)
Flatten it to a 1-D array of length 15
The array is fine; the real fix is to also pass a `shots` value

explanation.correct: For a parameter array, the trailing axis is matched against the circuit's free parameters, so its length must equal the number of parameters. The circuit has 2 parameters but the array's last axis is 3, hence the ValueError. Reshaping to (5, 2) makes the last axis 2 and binds 5 parameter sets, giving 5 results.

distractors.B: (3, 5) has a last axis of 5, still not equal to 2 parameters, so it raises the same error.
distractors.C: A flat length-15 array has last axis 15 != 2; it does not encode 2-parameter sets and still fails.
distractors.D: The error is about the parameter-axis length, not shots. Adding shots leaves the (5, 3) shape mismatch in place.

evidence.A: reshape to (5,2) so last axis = 2 params: SUCCESS, result shape (5,)
evidence.B:
transpose to (3,5): raised ValueError: Length of ('p[0]', 'p[1]') inconsistent with last dimension of [[0. 0. 0. 0. 0.]
 [0. 0. 0
evidence.C: flatten to 1-D length 15: raised ValueError: Length of ('p[0]', 'p[1]') inconsistent with last dimension of [0. 0. 0. 0. 0. 0. 0. 0. 0.
evidence.D:
keeping (5,3) and adding shots: still raised ValueError: Length of ('p[0]', 'p[1]') inconsistent with last dimension of [[0. 0. 0.]
 [0.

questions.stem: You want to switch on dynamical decoupling (an error-suppression technique that fills idle qubit time) for a `SamplerV2` called `sampler`. Which line does this correctly?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
# enable dynamical decoupling:

options.text: `sampler.options.dynamical_decoupling.enable = True`
`sampler.options.dynamical_decoupling = True`
`sampler.run([isa], dynamical_decoupling=True)`
`sampler.options.resilience.dynamical_decoupling = True`

explanation.correct: Dynamical decoupling is configured through the nested options object: `options.dynamical_decoupling` is an options group whose `.enable` flag turns the feature on. Setting `options.dynamical_decoupling.enable = True` is the supported form.

distractors.B: `dynamical_decoupling` is an options *group*, not a boolean. Assigning `True` to the whole group fails validation — you must set its `.enable` field.
distractors.C: `run()` does not accept a `dynamical_decoupling` keyword; options are configured on `sampler.options`, not passed to `run()`. This raises a TypeError.
distractors.D: SamplerV2 has no `resilience` options group (that concept lives on the Estimator). Accessing `options.resilience` fails; DD lives directly under `options.dynamical_decoupling`.

evidence.A: set options.dynamical_decoupling.enable=True: OK, enable now True, job ran
evidence.B:
raised ValidationError: 2 validation errors for SamplerOptions
dynamical_decoupling.DynamicalDecouplingOptions
  I
evidence.C: raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() got an unexpected keyword argument 'dynamical_decoupling'
evidence.D: raised AttributeError: 'SamplerOptions' object has no attribute 'resilience'

questions.stem: Which of the following are settable options on a `SamplerV2` primitive? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: `sampler.options.default_shots`
`sampler.options.dynamical_decoupling.enable`
`sampler.options.twirling.enable_gates`
`sampler.options.resilience_level`
`sampler.options.optimization_level`

explanation.correct: SamplerV2 options cover sampling and error-handling: `default_shots`, the `dynamical_decoupling` group (error suppression), and the `twirling` group (Pauli/measurement twirling), plus execution and simulator settings. `resilience_level` is an EstimatorV2 option, and `optimization_level` is a transpiler setting (used when building the pass manager) — neither exists on the Sampler, so setting them raises a validation error.

distractors.D: `resilience_level` selects Estimator mitigation strategies for expectation values; the Sampler returns raw samples and has no such option.
distractors.E: `optimization_level` is a transpiler argument (e.g. to `generate_preset_pass_manager`), not a primitive option. The Sampler validates and rejects it.

evidence.A: options.default_shots = 2048: accepted (valid SamplerV2 option)
evidence.B: options.dynamical_decoupling.enable = True: accepted (valid SamplerV2 option)
evidence.C: options.twirling.enable_gates = True: accepted (valid SamplerV2 option)
evidence.D:
options.resilience_level = 1: rejected -> ValidationError: 1 validation error for SamplerOptions
resilience_level
  Object has no attribute
evidence.E:
options.optimization_level = 3: rejected -> ValidationError: 1 validation error for SamplerOptions
optimization_level
  Object has no attribu

questions.stem: A research team wants complete control over readout-error correction: they need the untouched per-shot measurement outcomes so they can apply their own calibration matrix afterward. Which statement about SamplerV2 is correct?

options.text: SamplerV2 applies no readout-error mitigation to its output by default; it returns the raw per-shot bitstrings (a BitArray), so any custom mitigation can be done in post-processing
SamplerV2 always applies TREX readout mitigation, so obtaining raw outcomes is impossible without editing the runtime source
You must set `resilience_level = 0` on the sampler to disable its built-in mitigation before raw counts are returned
Bypassing mitigation requires switching to EstimatorV2, which is the only primitive that exposes raw data

explanation.correct: The Sampler primitive is deliberately low-level: it returns the sampled bitstrings as a BitArray with no post-processing correction of measurement error applied by default. That raw per-shot data is exactly what you need to implement your own mitigation (e.g. build and apply a readout-calibration/confusion matrix, or use a package like mthree). Error suppression (dynamical decoupling) and twirling can be opted into, but they do not alter the counts into 'mitigated' values.

distractors.B: TREX is an Estimator resilience technique for expectation values; the Sampler does not apply it, so raw outcomes are simply what you already get.
distractors.C: The Sampler has no `resilience_level` at all, so there is nothing to set to 0 — the returned counts are already raw.
distractors.D: It is the Sampler, not the Estimator, that exposes per-shot samples. The Estimator returns processed expectation values, which is the opposite of raw data.

questions.stem: You have a parameterized ISA circuit `isa`, a NumPy array `vals` of parameter values, and want 256 shots. Which call passes a correctly-structured SamplerV2 PUB?
questions.code:
# isa: parameterized ISA circuit ; vals: array of parameter values
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
job = sampler.run( ??? )

options.text: `sampler.run([(isa, vals, 256)])`
`sampler.run([(isa, 256, vals)])`
`sampler.run([(isa, observable, vals, 0.02)])`
`sampler.run((isa, vals))`

explanation.correct: A SamplerV2 PUB is a tuple `(circuit, parameter_values, shots)` where the last two are optional, and the run() argument is a *list* of such PUBs. `(isa, vals, 256)` places parameter values in slot 2 and shots in slot 3, wrapped in a list — the correct form.

distractors.B: This swaps the order: slot 2 is parameter_values and slot 3 is shots, so `(isa, 256, vals)` tries to use the array as the shot count and raises a TypeError ('shots must be an integer').
distractors.C: A 4-element tuple with an observable is the *Estimator* PUB `(circuit, observable, params, precision)`. The Sampler takes no observable; a PUB length of 4 is rejected ('length of pub must be 1, 2 or 3').
distractors.D: run() expects a list of PUBs. Passing a bare tuple makes run() iterate it as if `isa` and `vals` were two separate PUBs, which fails. The PUB must be wrapped: `run([(isa, vals)])`.

evidence.A: (isa, vals, 256): SUCCESS, shape=(3,), num_shots=256
evidence.B: (isa, 256, vals): raised TypeError: shots must be an integer
evidence.C: (isa, obs, vals, 0.02): raised ValueError: The length of pub must be 1, 2 or 3, but length 4 is given.
evidence.D: run((isa, vals)): raised ValueError: The number of values (0) does not match the number of parameters (1) for the cir

questions.stem: A 1-parameter circuit is broadcast over 4 parameter values at 500 shots each. What does `sum(result[0].data.meas.get_counts().values())` return when `get_counts()` is called with no index?
questions.code:
import numpy as np

vals = np.array([[0.0], [np.pi/3], [np.pi/2], [np.pi]])   # 4 parameter sets
ba = sampler.run([(isa, vals)], shots=500).result()[0].data.meas
print(sum(ba.get_counts().values()))

options.text: `2000` — with no index, `get_counts()` aggregates the shots from all 4 parameter sets (4 x 500)
`500` — it returns the counts for just the first parameter set
`4` — one entry per parameter set
`125` — the 500 shots are divided among the 4 parameter sets

explanation.correct: On a broadcast (non-scalar) BitArray, `get_counts()` with no index flattens across every broadcast entry and tallies all of their shots together: 4 parameter sets x 500 shots = 2000. To get the histogram for a single parameter set you must index, e.g. `get_counts(0)`.

distractors.B: Without an index it does not pick the first set; it aggregates all of them. `get_counts(0)` would be needed to isolate the first set's 500 shots.
distractors.C: get_counts() returns a histogram dict (bitstring -> count), not one entry per parameter set. The 4 is the broadcast length, not the aggregate total.
distractors.D: Each parameter set runs the full 500 shots independently; shots are not split across the sweep. The aggregate is 4 x 500 = 2000.

evidence.A: option claims 2000; ba.get_counts() (no index) totalled 2000 over shape (4,) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims 500; ba.get_counts() (no index) totalled 2000 over shape (4,) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims 4; ba.get_counts() (no index) totalled 2000 over shape (4,) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims 125; ba.get_counts() (no index) totalled 2000 over shape (4,) -> match=False

questions.stem: A circuit measures into two named classical registers, `alice` and `bob`. After running the Sampler, how do you read the counts for the `alice` register?
questions.code:
qr = QuantumRegister(2, 'q')
alice = ClassicalRegister(1, 'alice')
bob   = ClassicalRegister(1, 'bob')
qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, alice, bob)
# ... measure into alice and bob, transpile, run ...
result = sampler.run([isa]).result()

options.text: `result[0].data.alice.get_counts()`
`result[0].data.meas.get_counts()['alice']`
`result[0].get_counts('alice')`
`result.data.alice.get_counts()`

explanation.correct: Each classical register becomes its own field in the result's DataBin, named after the register. With registers `alice` and `bob`, the DataBin has `.alice` and `.bob` BitArrays, so `result[0].data.alice.get_counts()` returns that register's histogram.

distractors.B: There is no `meas` field here — `meas` only appears when you use `measure_all()`. With named registers the fields are `alice` and `bob`, so `data.meas` raises AttributeError.
distractors.C: The PUB result has no `get_counts()` method that takes a register name; counts live on the per-register BitArray reached via `.data.<register>`.
distractors.D: `result` is a PrimitiveResult (a sequence of PUB results). You must index the PUB first with `result[0]` before reaching `.data`.

evidence.A: result[0].data.alice.get_counts() -> {'0': 12, '1': 188}
evidence.B: result[0].data.meas: raised AttributeError: 'DataBin' object has no attribute 'meas'
evidence.C: result[0].get_counts('alice'): raised AttributeError: 'SamplerPubResult' object has no attribute 'get_counts'
evidence.D: result.data (no [0]): raised AttributeError: 'PrimitiveResult' object has no attribute 'data'

questions.stem: This code raises `AttributeError: 'DataBin' object has no attribute 'meas'` on the last line. What is the correct fix?
questions.code:
qc = QuantumCircuit(2, 2)
qc.h(0); qc.cx(0, 1)
qc.measure([0, 1], [0, 1])
# ... transpile to isa, run ...
result = sampler.run([isa]).result()
counts = result[0].data.meas.get_counts()   # AttributeError

options.text: The register created by `QuantumCircuit(2, 2)` is named `c`, so use `result[0].data.c.get_counts()`
Replace the explicit measurement with `qc.measure_all()` — `data.meas` only works alongside `measure(...)`
`BitArray` has no `get_counts()`; iterate `get_bitstrings()` and tally them yourself
You must pass `shots=` to `run()` so the `meas` field gets created

explanation.correct: The DataBin field name comes from the classical register's name. `QuantumCircuit(2, 2)` creates a default register named `c`, so the outcomes are in `data.c`, not `data.meas`. The name `meas` only exists if the circuit used `measure_all()`, which auto-creates a register called `meas`.

distractors.B: This inverts the truth: `measure_all()` is exactly what creates a `meas` register. With the explicit `measure(...)` here, the field is `c`. Rewriting the circuit is unnecessary — just use `data.c`.
distractors.C: `BitArray.get_counts()` does exist and works fine; the error is only about the field name, not a missing method.
distractors.D: Shots were run regardless; the field name depends on the register name (`c`), not on the shot count. Passing shots does not create a `meas` field.

evidence.B: circuit uses QuantumCircuit(2,2); its register is 'c' not 'meas'. data.meas -> AttributeError: 'DataBin' object has no attribute 'meas'
evidence.C: BitArray.get_counts() exists and works = True; claim that get_counts is unavailable is false
evidence.D: shots=200 was passed yet field is 'c', not 'meas'; fields=['c']

questions.stem: You set `sampler.options.default_shots = 1000`, then submit a single PUB that carries its own shot count: `sampler.run([(isa, None, 50)])`. How many shots does that PUB run?
questions.code:
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
sampler.options.default_shots = 1000
job = sampler.run([(isa, None, 50)])   # PUB = (circuit, params, shots)
num = job.result()[0].data.meas.num_shots

options.text: 50 — a shot count given in the PUB overrides the `default_shots` option
1000 — the `default_shots` option always wins over a PUB value
1050 — the PUB shots are added to the default
500 — the two settings are averaged

explanation.correct: Shots resolve most-specific-first: a value inside the PUB (slot 3) takes precedence over `options.default_shots`, which itself takes precedence over the built-in 1024 fallback. Here the PUB specifies 50, so exactly 50 shots run despite `default_shots = 1000`.

distractors.B: The default is only a fallback for PUBs that omit shots. A PUB that carries its own shot count overrides it, so 50 wins, not 1000.
distractors.C: Shot settings are not additive; the more specific PUB value replaces the default rather than summing with it.
distractors.D: There is no averaging of shot counts. Precedence is a strict override, giving 50.

evidence.A: option claims 50 shots; with default_shots=1000 and PUB shots=50, observed num_shots=50 -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims 1000 shots; with default_shots=1000 and PUB shots=50, observed num_shots=50 -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims 1050 shots; with default_shots=1000 and PUB shots=50, observed num_shots=50 -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims 500 shots; with default_shots=1000 and PUB shots=50, observed num_shots=50 -> match=False

questions.stem: Which of the following are real fields of the SamplerV2 `twirling` options group? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: `twirling.enable_gates`
`twirling.enable_measure`
`twirling.num_randomizations`
`twirling.twirling_level`
`twirling.readout`

explanation.correct: The twirling options group controls Pauli twirling of gates and measurements: `enable_gates` and `enable_measure` toggle each kind, `num_randomizations` sets how many random twirls to average over, and `shots_per_randomization`/`strategy` complete the group. `twirling_level` and `readout` are not fields of this group, so assigning them raises a validation error.

distractors.D: There is no `twirling_level`. Twirling is toggled per-kind (`enable_gates`, `enable_measure`) and tuned via `num_randomizations`; there is no single integer 'level'.
distractors.E: Readout/measurement twirling is controlled by `enable_measure`, not a field called `readout`. Setting `twirling.readout` fails validation.

evidence.A: twirling.enable_gates = True: accepted (real field)
evidence.B: twirling.enable_measure = True: accepted (real field)
evidence.C: twirling.num_randomizations = 8: accepted (real field)
evidence.D:
twirling.twirling_level: rejected -> ValidationError: 1 validation error for TwirlingOptions
twirling_level
  Object has no
evidence.E:
twirling.readout: rejected -> ValidationError: 1 validation error for TwirlingOptions
readout
  Object has no attribu

questions.stem: A Bell circuit should only ever yield `00` or `11`, but readout error sprinkles in `01`/`10`. A team wants to bypass runtime mitigation and apply their own scheme that keeps only the physically-consistent outcomes. Which approach implements this correctly?
questions.code:
# result from a Bell circuit; raw counts include 01/10 from readout error
counts = result[0].data.meas.get_counts()   # e.g. {'00':.., '11':.., '01':.., '10':..}

options.text: Filter the counts to keep only even-parity outcomes (`00`, `11`) and renormalize — a post-selection scheme you implement yourself on the returned data
Set `sampler.options.resilience_level = 2` so the runtime post-selects for you
Enable `dynamical_decoupling`, which removes the readout errors from the counts
Nothing can be done — you must accept the raw counts as the sampler returns them

explanation.correct: Because the Sampler hands back raw per-shot outcomes, you are free to implement any custom mitigation in post-processing. Here the known symmetry (a Bell state has even parity) lets you post-select: drop the `01`/`10` shots and renormalize over `00`/`11`. This is exactly the 'bypass runtime mitigation, roll your own' pattern.

distractors.B: SamplerV2 has no `resilience_level` — that is an Estimator option — so this line raises, and even resilience levels do not do parity post-selection.
distractors.C: Dynamical decoupling suppresses decoherence during idle periods in the circuit; it does not filter or correct measurement/readout outcomes, so leakage outcomes still appear in the counts.
distractors.D: The raw counts are precisely what makes custom mitigation possible; option A demonstrates a valid post-processing correction.

evidence.A: post-selecting even-parity kept 3763/4000 shots and discarded odd-parity leakage {'01': 90, '10': 147} -> user-implemented mitigation works = True
evidence.B:
resilience_level=2 raised ValidationError: 1 validation error for SamplerOptions
resilience_level
  Object has no (no such Sampler option)
evidence.C: with DD enabled, odd-parity readout outcomes still present (237 shots) -> DD does not post-select counts
evidence.D: post-processing IS possible (option A filtered 3763 shots) -> claim is false

questions.stem: A 2-qubit circuit is run with `shots=777`. What is `len(result[0].data.meas.get_bitstrings())`?
questions.code:
ba = sampler.run([isa], shots=777).result()[0].data.meas
print(len(ba.get_bitstrings()))

options.text: `777` — `get_bitstrings()` returns one entry per shot
`2` — one entry per qubit
`4` — one entry per possible 2-bit outcome
`1` — a single concatenated string of all shots

explanation.correct: `get_bitstrings()` returns the raw per-shot record: a list with exactly one bitstring for every shot, in order. With 777 shots you get a list of length 777. (`get_counts()` is the summarized view; `get_bitstrings()` is the un-aggregated one.)

distractors.B: The length is per-shot, not per-qubit. Each entry is a full 2-character bitstring; there are as many entries as shots.
distractors.C: 4 is the number of *possible* outcomes; `get_bitstrings()` lists actual shots (with repeats), so its length is the shot count.
distractors.D: It returns a list of separate bitstrings (one per shot), not one giant concatenated string.

evidence.A: option claims len=777; len(get_bitstrings())=777 (num_shots=777) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims len=2; len(get_bitstrings())=777 (num_shots=777) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims len=4; len(get_bitstrings())=777 (num_shots=777) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims len=1; len(get_bitstrings())=777 (num_shots=777) -> match=False

questions.stem: In the primitives model, what does the Sampler primitive fundamentally return for a circuit that ends in measurements?

options.text: Samples drawn from the circuit's output probability distribution — per-shot measurement bitstrings that you can histogram into (quasi-)probabilities
The exact complex amplitudes of the circuit's output statevector
Expectation values of a set of observables
The single most-likely measurement outcome of the circuit

explanation.correct: 'Sampling' means repeatedly executing the circuit and recording the measured bitstring each shot — draws from the distribution the circuit defines over computational-basis states. The Sampler returns those per-shot samples (as a BitArray); estimating probabilities is then just counting frequencies. It never returns amplitudes or expectation values.

distractors.B: Amplitudes are hidden by measurement — sampling only sees classical outcomes with probabilities |amplitude|^2. Statevectors are available from simulators (quantum_info), not from a hardware Sampler.
distractors.C: Expectation values of observables are the job of the Estimator primitive, not the Sampler.
distractors.D: The Sampler returns the full collection of shot outcomes, not a single winner; a distribution over many shots is the whole point.

questions.stem: To make a SamplerV2 run on a fake/simulated backend reproducible — identical counts every time — which assignment sets the random seed correctly?
questions.code:
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=fake_backend)
# make results reproducible:

options.text: `sampler.options.simulator.seed_simulator = 42`
`sampler.options.seed = 42`
`sampler.run([isa], seed=42)`
`sampler.options.execution.seed_simulator = 42`

explanation.correct: The simulator seed lives in the `simulator` options group: `options.simulator.seed_simulator`. Fixing it makes the (fake) backend's internal simulator deterministic, so repeated runs return identical counts. Without it, each run draws fresh randomness.

distractors.B: There is no top-level `options.seed`; the seed is nested under the `simulator` group. This assignment fails validation.
distractors.C: `run()` has no `seed` keyword — seeding is an option on the primitive, not a run argument, so this raises a TypeError.
distractors.D: `seed_simulator` is a field of the `simulator` group, not `execution`. `options.execution` holds `init_qubits`/`rep_delay`/`meas_type`, and rejects `seed_simulator`.

evidence.A: two samplers with simulator.seed_simulator=42 -> counts identical = True ({'1': 968, '0': 1032} vs {'1': 968, '0': 1032})
evidence.B:
options.seed: raised ValidationError: 1 validation error for SamplerOptions
seed
  Object has no attribute ' (no such option)
evidence.C: run(seed=42): raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() got an unexpected keyword argument 'seed' (no seed kwarg)
evidence.D:
options.execution.seed_simulator: raised ValidationError: 1 validation error for SamplerExecutionOptionsV2
seed_simula (seed lives under simulator)

questions.stem: This code excites qubit 0 and tries to read qubit 0's value out of each shot's bitstring, but every `q0` comes out `'0'`. What is the fix?
questions.code:
qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.x(0)              # qubit 0 -> |1>
qc.measure_all()
# ... transpile, run ...
bitstrings = result[0].data.meas.get_bitstrings()
q0 = [b[0] for b in bitstrings]   # expected '1', gets '0'

options.text: Qiskit bitstrings are little-endian, so qubit 0 is the *last* character; read `b[-1]` instead of `b[0]`
Replace `measure_all()` with explicit `measure(0, 0); measure(1, 1)`, then `b[0]` reads qubit 0
Increase the shot count so the correct value stabilizes at `b[0]`
Read qubit 0 from `get_counts()` instead, whose keys index qubit 0 at position 0

explanation.correct: Qiskit prints bitstrings little-endian: the character at index 0 is the *highest*-indexed bit, and qubit 0 sits at the far right (index -1). Since only qubit 0 was excited, `b[-1]` is `'1'` while `b[0]` (qubit 1) is `'0'`. The fix is to index from the right.

distractors.B: The bit ordering is a display convention, not a consequence of `measure_all()`. Explicit measurement into bit 0 still places qubit 0 at the rightmost character, so `b[0]` still reads the wrong qubit.
distractors.C: This is a deterministic indexing error, not statistical noise. More shots do not move qubit 0 to index 0.
distractors.D: `get_counts()` keys are the same little-endian bitstrings, so index 0 of a key is still the highest qubit, not qubit 0.

evidence.A: reading bs[-1] gives '1' in 94.50% of shots vs bs[0] only 1.27% -> qubit 0 is the last char = True
evidence.B: after explicit measure(0,0), bs[0]=='1' in 1.27% of shots -> still not qubit 0 at index 0 = False
evidence.C: with 9000 shots, bs[0]=='1' in 1.06% -> more shots does not move qubit 0 to index 0 = False
evidence.D: dominant bitstring is '01'; its index-0 char is '0' -> get_counts/dominant[0] reads qubit 0 = False

questions.stem: You want every job from a sampler to use 4096 shots by default, without passing `shots` on each `run()`. Which construction sets this up correctly?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2
# want default_shots = 4096 for all runs

options.text: `SamplerV2(mode=backend, options={"default_shots": 4096})`
`SamplerV2(mode=backend, shots=4096)`
`SamplerV2(mode=backend, default_shots=4096)`
`s = SamplerV2(mode=backend); s.shots = 4096`

explanation.correct: The primitive constructor takes an `options` mapping (or a `SamplerOptions` object); `default_shots` is a key inside it. `SamplerV2(mode=backend, options={"default_shots": 4096})` makes every subsequent no-shots run use 4096. A per-run `shots=` argument would still override it.

distractors.B: There is no `shots` parameter on the SamplerV2 constructor; shots are a run argument or the `default_shots` option. This raises a TypeError.
distractors.C: `default_shots` is not a top-level constructor keyword either — it lives inside the `options` mapping. Passing it directly raises a TypeError.
distractors.D: SamplerV2 has no `shots` attribute controlling defaults; assigning `s.shots` just sets an unused Python attribute, so no-shots runs still fall back to 1024.

evidence.A: SamplerV2(mode=backend, options={"default_shots":4096}) -> a no-shots run used 4096 shots = True
evidence.B: SamplerV2(shots=4096): raised TypeError: SamplerV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'shots'
evidence.C: SamplerV2(default_shots=4096): raised TypeError: SamplerV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'default_shots
evidence.D: setting sampler.shots=4096 then no-shots run used 1024 shots -> took effect = False

questions.stem: Which statements about error handling in SamplerV2 are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: By default SamplerV2 applies no measurement-error mitigation; the returned counts are raw samples
Dynamical decoupling is an error-*suppression* technique you can turn on via `options.dynamical_decoupling.enable`
Setting `options.resilience_level` selects the sampler's built-in mitigation strategy
Twirling reduces the number of qubits that get measured
Because the sampler post-processes the data internally, you cannot add your own mitigation

explanation.correct: SamplerV2 returns raw samples with no automatic readout mitigation (A), and its error-handling knobs are error *suppression* (dynamical decoupling) and twirling — DD is enabled through `options.dynamical_decoupling.enable` (B). Everything downstream (readout calibration, post-selection) is left to you.

distractors.C: `resilience_level` is an EstimatorV2 option for expectation-value mitigation; SamplerV2 has no such option, so it cannot select any built-in mitigation.
distractors.D: Twirling inserts randomized Pauli/measurement operations to tailor noise; it does not change how many qubits are measured.
distractors.E: The opposite is true: the sampler returns un-mitigated per-shot data precisely so you *can* apply your own mitigation afterward.

questions.stem: You broadcast a 1-parameter circuit over 6 parameter values in one PUB and pass `shots=200`. How many total circuit executions does the sampler perform?
questions.code:
import numpy as np
vals = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 6).reshape(6, 1)   # 6 parameter sets
job = sampler.run([(isa, vals)], shots=200)

options.text: 1200 — every parameter set gets its own 200 shots (6 x 200)
200 — the 200 shots are shared across the whole sweep
6 — one execution per parameter value
34 — 200 shots divided among the 6 parameter sets

explanation.correct: `shots` is per parameter set, not per PUB total. Broadcasting over 6 parameter values means the circuit is sampled 200 times at each of the 6 bindings, for 6 x 200 = 1200 executions. Each set's counts independently sum to 200.

distractors.B: The shot count applies to each broadcast element, so the sweep does not share a single 200-shot budget; the total is 6 x 200.
distractors.C: 6 is the number of parameter sets, not executions. Each set runs 200 shots, so executions number 1200.
distractors.D: Shots are not divided across the sweep; each parameter set receives the full 200 shots.

evidence.A: option claims 1200; summed shots over all 6 parameter sets = 1200 (6 x 200) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims 200; summed shots over all 6 parameter sets = 1200 (6 x 200) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims 6; summed shots over all 6 parameter sets = 1200 (6 x 200) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims 34; summed shots over all 6 parameter sets = 1200 (6 x 200) -> match=False

questions.stem: A 2-qubit circuit applies X to both qubits and measures all. What is the dominant key of `result[0].data.meas.get_int_counts()`?
questions.code:
qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.x(0); qc.x(1)
qc.measure_all()
# ... run ...
ba = result[0].data.meas
print(ba.get_int_counts())   # dominant key?

options.text: `3` — `get_int_counts()` keys are integers; the bitstring `11` is binary 3
`'11'` — same string keys as `get_counts()`
`11` — the digits `11` read as the decimal number eleven
`2` — two qubits are set to 1

explanation.correct: `get_int_counts()` returns the same histogram as `get_counts()` but with each bitstring decoded as an integer. Both qubits are 1, so the outcome bitstring is `11`, which as a binary number is 3. The keys are true integers (3), not the string `'11'`.

distractors.B: That is `get_counts()`'s output (string keys). `get_int_counts()` converts each key to an integer, so `'11'` becomes `3`.
distractors.C: The bitstring is interpreted in binary, not read as base-10 digits, so `11` is 2+1 = 3, not eleven.
distractors.D: 2 would be the *count* of set qubits, not the integer value of the outcome. Binary `11` equals 3.

evidence.A: option claims dominant get_int_counts() key 3 (int); observed 3 (int) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims dominant get_int_counts() key '11' (str); observed 3 (int) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims dominant get_int_counts() key 11 (int); observed 3 (int) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims dominant get_int_counts() key 2 (int); observed 3 (int) -> match=False

questions.stem: Having chosen not to rely on any runtime mitigation, a team wants to rigorously correct measurement (readout) error in their SamplerV2 counts — not merely post-select. Which do-it-yourself technique fits the raw per-shot data the Sampler provides?

options.text: Characterize readout with calibration circuits to build an assignment (confusion) matrix, then apply its inverse to the measured counts (e.g. a matrix-based method such as M3)
Raise `sampler.options.resilience_level` until the readout error disappears
Apply Zero-Noise Extrapolation directly to the bitstrings
Turn on gate twirling, which mathematically inverts the readout confusion matrix

explanation.correct: Readout error is a classical relabeling of measured bits, captured by an assignment/confusion matrix measured from calibration circuits (prepare each basis state, see what you read). Inverting (or least-squares inverting) that matrix corrects the counts — the approach used by tools like mthree (M3). The Sampler's raw counts are exactly the input such a method needs.

distractors.B: SamplerV2 has no `resilience_level`; that Estimator option cannot be set here, and it would not build a readout-calibration matrix anyway.
distractors.C: Zero-Noise Extrapolation mitigates errors in *expectation values* by scaling gate noise (an Estimator technique); it is not defined on raw sampled bitstrings.
distractors.D: Twirling randomizes coherent gate/measurement noise into a simpler stochastic form; it does not measure or invert the readout confusion matrix.

questions.stem: You estimate an outcome probability from a SamplerV2 histogram and then increase the shot count from 1000 to 4000. Roughly how does the statistical (sampling) error on that estimated probability change?

options.text: It roughly halves — sampling (shot) error scales like 1/sqrt(N), so 4x the shots gives about 2x smaller error
It drops to a quarter, scaling like 1/N with the number of shots
It is essentially unchanged — statistical error depends on the hardware, not the shot count
It roughly doubles, because more shots accumulate more noise

explanation.correct: Counting outcomes is a Monte-Carlo estimate of a probability. The standard error of a frequency is sqrt(p(1-p)/N), i.e. it shrinks like 1/sqrt(N). Quadrupling the shots (1000 -> 4000) multiplies the error by 1/sqrt(4) = 1/2. This is why halving the error costs 4x the shots — the same shots<->precision trade-off that underlies the primitives.

distractors.B: Sampling error scales like 1/sqrt(N), not 1/N. You would need 16x the shots (not 4x) to quarter the error.
distractors.C: Shot noise is a property of finite sampling and clearly decreases with more shots; it is not fixed by the hardware.
distractors.D: More shots reduce, not increase, the statistical uncertainty of the estimate; the error goes down like 1/sqrt(N).

questions.stem: You submit three circuits in one call: `result = sampler.run([isaA, isaB, isaC]).result()`. What is `len(result)`?
questions.code:
result = sampler.run([isaA, isaB, isaC]).result()
print(len(result))

options.text: 3 — one PUB result per submitted PUB, indexed `result[0]`, `result[1]`, `result[2]`
1 — all circuits are merged into a single result object
256 — one entry per shot
768 — total shots across the three circuits

explanation.correct: `run()` takes a list of PUBs and returns a PrimitiveResult that is a sequence of PUB results, one per input PUB, in order. Three circuits in, three results out — you read each with `result[i].data.<register>.get_counts()`.

distractors.B: Results are not merged; each PUB keeps its own result object. `len(result)` equals the number of PUBs, here 3.
distractors.C: The length is the number of PUBs, not shots. Shots live inside each PUB result's BitArray (`num_shots`).
distractors.D: 768 is 3 x 256 total shots, but `len(result)` counts PUB results (3), not shots.

evidence.A: option claims len(result)=3; run([isaA,isaB,isaC]) gave len(result)=3 -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims len(result)=1; run([isaA,isaB,isaC]) gave len(result)=3 -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims len(result)=256; run([isaA,isaB,isaC]) gave len(result)=3 -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims len(result)=768; run([isaA,isaB,isaC]) gave len(result)=3 -> match=False
